---
title: "Combining Sources"
description: "Data.provider loads several sources in parallel; Data.Merger joins tables you've already loaded by a shared key — when to use which."
---

data.js has two different tools for working with more than one source, solving two different problems:

- **`Data.provider()`** — you have several URLs to *load*, and want one callback once they've all resolved.
- **`Data.Merger`** — you already have two or more loaded tables, and want to *join* them by a shared key column.

## `Data.provider()`: load several sources in parallel

```javascript
Data.provider()
    .addSource("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ondata/elezionipolitiche2018/master/dati/scrutiniCI_cm.csv", "csv")
    .addSource("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ondata/elezionipolitiche2018/master/risorse/comuniViminaleISTAT.csv", "csv")
    .addSource("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ondata/elezionipolitiche2018/master/dati/camera_geopolitico_italia.csv", "csv")
    .load(function (dataA) {
        var scrutini = dataA[0];
        var comuniViminaleISTAT = dataA[1];
        var cameraGeopoliticoItalia = dataA[2];

        scrutini = scrutini.select('WHERE tipo_riga == "LI"');
        // ... continue working with all three tables
    });
```

`load(callback)` fires once with an array of `Data.Table`, in the same order you called `addSource`. Use `.error(handler)` for a shared load-failure handler, and `.notify(handler)` if you want a progress callback as each source starts/finishes.

:::{.callout-note}
## `.load()` is an alias for `.realize()`
Both names fire the same way — `.load()` matches `Data.feed(...).load(callback)`'s naming and is the one to reach for in new code. `.realize()` is the older name and still works exactly the same.
:::

:::{.callout-note}
## `Data.provider()` is the preferred name — `Data.Broker` is the older one
`Data.provider()` and `Data.broker()` are two names for the same thing — both return a `Data.Broker` instance. `Data.provider()` is the name to reach for in new code; `Data.Broker`/`Data.broker()` still work and show up in older examples and in some internal method names (`Data.Feed.prototype.broker()`), but are the legacy naming.
:::

:::{.callout-important}
## Doesn't inherit `Data.Table`/`Data.Feed` methods
Despite an internal comment suggesting otherwise, a `Data.provider()` instance doesn't expose `.select()`/`.column()` directly — work with the individual tables in the `.load()` callback's array instead.
:::

## `Data.Merger`: join already-loaded tables

Use this once you have two or more `Data.Table`s (from separate `Data.feed()` calls, or from a `Data.provider()`) and want to combine them by a shared key:

```javascript
var merger = new Data.Merger()
    .addSource(prezzi,   { lookup: "idImpianto", columns: ["descCarburante", "prezzo", "isSelf", "dtComu"] })
    .addSource(impianti, { lookup: "idImpianto", columns: ["Bandiera", "Latitudine", "Longitudine"] });

merger.realize(function (mergedTable) {
    var selection = mergedTable.select('WHERE tipo_riga == "LI"');
    // ... continue working with the merged table
});
```

The **first** `addSource` call provides the row backbone; every subsequent source is looked up by matching `lookup` values and its `columns` are added alongside. `label` (optional, in the same `addSource` options object) lets you rename incoming columns; `setOutputColumns([...])` afterwards restricts/orders the final table to specific columns.

| | `Data.provider()` | `Data.Merger` |
|---|---|---|
| Input | URLs (loads them itself) | Already-loaded `Data.Table`s (or plain 2D arrays) |
| Combines by | Nothing — just returns all tables together | A shared `lookup` key, join-style |
| Use when | You need several independent datasets loaded before continuing | You need to enrich one table's rows with columns from another |

---

Next: see the full [API Reference](api_reference.qmd) for every method's exact signature.
